sentences.text: [BEEPING]
So I knew what had happened to my mother up to the beginning of September, but not where she was.
Then we heard that Mrs.
Nowodworska and her daughter, who were with my mother at that time, had been deported to Germany-- but still no news of Mother.
And what-- again, same story as before-- lots of people coming to the estate.
So in the end, I was quartered in the village with Mrs.
Nowodworska's mother, her sister, younger son, and myself, in a peasant's house.
That would be, I think that would be probably end of October, beginning of November.
And at that time, Mrs.
Biedrzycka who was the owner of, wife of the owner of the estate, got a letter from my mother, asking what had happened to Mrs.
Nowodworska and her daughter, because they had been separated in [?
Puschkau. ?] So that was how we found each other, because they wrote back and said that Mrs.
Nowodworska and her daughter were in Germany, but I was there.
They knew my story.
I was there.
And if she wanted to join me, she was welcome.
So she arrived, and we were reunited.
That was November, four months, virtually, from the time we last saw each other.
No, I can't.
I can't remember.
All I know is that I was rich, because I had some material, which-- when during the Rising, we captured some German stores.
And there were different materials.
And the boys cut up some navy and white fabrics and gave us, to the girls, for uniforms after the war.
And I had that in my knapsack, so I sold that, bought myself some wool.
But it wasn't normal wool, because during the war, there was very little wool.
And wool was made out of tree bark.
It was wood wool.
I taught myself to knit, made myself a jumper and socks, then made a jumper for my mother when she came.
And then we, we moved into another peasant's house-- or just the two of us.
They were strange times, because we had no money at all.
So we were getting some food from the estate.
And I was, drew Christmas cards, painted Christmas cards, which I sold in the village [? corp. ?] We used to go around with a pushcart, asking for brown coal for heating.
I was giving French lessons, [LAUGHS] all sorts of things, because we had to eat.
You would hear that, you know, the butcher in the village or in the nearest town had sold, had killed a pig.
So you would go there, and sometimes they would give you something.
But that involved going through the woods.
And there were partisans in the woods of different groupings.
In fact, in January, '45, Mrs.
Biedrzycka husband and son-in-law were murdered by the Communist partisans.
So life was not very easy.
I was quite [? a dumbhead ?] at baking bread at that time.
But we very much had to rely on ourselves.
Another source of funds was that in the ghetto, I started having some orthodontic work down.
And I had four gold crowns.
Well, the orthodontic work did not proceed, so the crowns were irrelevant.
So we had them removed and sold.
And gave us some-- that way, we had a bit of money to buy essentials.
And then in January, the Russians arrived.
Can you remember where you were when they arrived to [INAUDIBLE]?
Yes, we were in that village, so [INAUDIBLE].
They arrived.
It was quite strange, because there were lorry loads of soldiers going.
And all they had to say was [INAUDIBLE] Berlin, which was, where is Berlin?
And they were miles from Berlin, but they were completely-- [INAUDIBLE]-- illiterate.
They had no idea.
They were just on their way to Berlin, and where is Berlin?
Distance didn't matter.
And then, when they, they came back--
Well, they were on their way.
Everybody was very scared of them, because there were tales of their raping women.
So again, I had to be dressed in [INAUDIBLE] with soot on my face, and head scarves, and so on and so forth.
But nothing happened in the village.
But they moved the family from the manor house.
They occupied the manor house, and things changed.
Then they came back.
And they were very good.
I had my first-- they had lorry loads of loot from Germany.
I can't remember.
Probably a couple of weeks-- two, three weeks.
I got a blouse, which is, which I'm wearing in the photograph which I have shown to you.
It was a red and black check blouse.
I still remember.
It was the first new piece of clothing.
I mean, it wasn't new.
But it was new to me.
And they gave us some blankets.
I don't remember.
I don't think so.
But blankets were very welcome.
And we stayed in this village till it got a bit warmer.
I think would have been probably March or early April, even, when we decided to go back to Warsaw.
And again, we hitchhiked.
My mother and I, yes.
And on one of the lifts we had, there were some French prisoners of war going back.
And my mother's sister is married to a Frenchman, or was married to a Frenchman, and lived in France.
So my mother wrote a little note and asked the French soldier to take it back and post it and tell her that he had seen us.
Unfortunately, he did write a note.
But he said he had seen a French-speaking lady.
He didn't mention me.
So nobody knew that I was with my mother, and I was alive.
We got to Warsaw.
And that was a scene of utter devastation.
Most of Warsaw had been bombed during the Warsaw Rising.
And when the Rising collapsed in October, the Germans systematically went round, setting fire to the houses which were still standing.
So there were very few habitable houses left.
We found my grandmother's cook and my grandfather's assistant, butler, or whatever you called him, who lived on the Praga side of Warsaw, where the Russians were in when the Warsaw Rising started.
So that part of Warsaw was not involved in the fighting.
We went to them.
And they gave us shelter.
My grandmother's cook put us in business.
She knew somebody who was making soap.
Well, you wouldn't want to use that soap, but-- said she can arrange for us to have samples to sell without a deposit, because we had no money.
Well, my mother wasn't a very good saleswoman, because when she was told that the soap was so wet, it was practically useless, she agreed.
[LAUGHTER] I was better at it.
But it ended sadly, because I left the samples on the windowsill in the sun, and they dried out.
The result was that they were unsellable, and that was the end of my sales career.
But, you know, we had a floor to sleep on, and that was all you could have.
We really were very, very short of money, to the extent that we would, we went to the soup kitchen as a Jewish relief, whatever it was.
We also said, gave our names, so that they could be broadcast that we had survived.
But I remember an occasion-- probably more than one occasion-- when we went to buy a plate of soup in [? a cafe ?], and asked for a plate of soup and two plates.
Sorry-- a plate of soup and two spoons, because we couldn't afford any more than that.
Well, there was nothing there to go to.
What for?
Was nothing there.
There was nothing left of our apartments.
I mean, in fact, my grandparents' apartment was standing, but you know, I don't know who was there.
There wouldn't have been anything of ours left.
No, I don't-- No, because everything was lost in the ghetto.
I think whatever there was which we-- my mother took, and my grandparents took.
As I said, the bracelet, which, I was told, weighed half a kilo, I left behind, cleverly, probably was taken off my grandmother in Treblinka.
So there was, there was nothing to go back to.
We just had what we were standing in.
Well, one day, we were walking in Warsaw.
And suddenly, a car stopped.
And it was the a pre-war friend of my parents, Gustaw Werfel, who recognized Mother and was delighted to see her, asked what we were doing and what was happening to us.
And I think he might, he was deported to Russia.
Yes.
In any case, he was a director of the Polish Repatriation Institute, which was in Lodz.
And he said to Mother, OK, move there.
And I'll get you a job.
Well, my mother had never worked in all her life.
But one has to live.
So we went to Lodz.
At first, we, again, we stayed in different floors, whomever we met who would give us the chance of sleeping in their room.
Because people just had rooms, not apartments anymore.
And Mother got a job as a bookkeeper.
And I, the first thing I did, I went to school.
I enrolled.
That was May, '45.
I decided I was going back to school, so I enrolled at school.
And when I-- in the evenings, I had to help mother with her bookkeeping.
She used to bring the books home.
I was better at figures, so we did the books together.
And then the, the summer holidays start in June.
So I got a job, again, in the accounts department with her.
I had to have a medical.
And the doctor took one look at me and said, I think you would do better with a holiday, to which Mother said, well, if she doesn't work, we don't eat very well.
So I got a job for the summer in the accounts department.
And in September, I went back to school.
Well, Mother wrote to whomever she could think of-- you know, to the Swiss address, to her sister in France, to friends in Brazil-- looking for news.
But it was very difficult.
And in fact, the letters which she wrote, although they got to my father, in the end, the sad thing was that everybody managed somehow to miss my name off them.
So Father knew that she had survived, but he had no idea what had happened to me.
And as soon as the war ended, he was in-- at that time, he was in Italy, with the second Polish corps, which was part of the Eighth Army.
He had done the Italian campaign, Monte Cassino, and all that.
He and a friend took a Jeep and went to Germany to DP camps and to prisoner of war camps, armed with my photograph, trying to find out if anybody knew anything about me.
In one POW camp, they found somebody who had known me in the Warsaw Rising, who said, well, she was alive at the end of August, but don't know what happened after that.
And then finally, he found Mrs.
Wedecka's daughter in a POW camp, where she was taken at the end of the Warsaw Rising, when the Germans recognized the Polish Home Army, gave them prisoner of war rights under the Geneva Convention.
And she said that she knew that I had survived and had been taken out of Warsaw.
And then I don't know at which point we heard, but it must have been about September, October, that we had news that Father was alive.
But we assumed that so was George.
And then one day, beginning of November, '45, we were in the room we were renting, when two people turned up, a man and a woman, Jewish, with a note in my father's handwriting, saying, trust them.
I'm waiting for you.
Then my mother said, you know, what is the plan?
He said, well, they was, they are collecting a group of people.
And they will guide us out of Poland.
And my father's waiting in Linz.
It was-- In Austria?
In Austria.
And they will get us there.
There was no question.
We just up and left.

And the group consisted of my mother and myself, the wife of a Polish officer, a friend of my father's, another wife of an officer with a six-year-old daughter, and a boy of 14, the orphaned son of my father's commanding officer, whose mother had died during the war.
So it was a sort of slightly ill-assorted group.
What we hadn't realized-- or I'm sure my father didn't know-- that these people had no means of getting us out of Poland, other than what I would call chutzpah.
First, they took us to Katowice, to some Jewish organization which was smuggling survivors to Israel.
But of course, maybe by some stretch, we could have qualified, but nobody else in the group.
So we left there.
And we went to a border town with Czechoslovakia, where our guides proceeded to try and hitchhike out of-- with us out of Poland, stopping Russian lorries and trying to bribe them to give us a lift.
Does Linz-- is it the other side of Czechoslovakia, isn't it?
That's right.
Well, they were supposed to take us to the, to the frontier.
Well, we finally got a lift.
We got on a Russian lorry, were hidden in the back.
The Russians said not to worry, the Poles will not stop them at the frontier, drove into Czechoslovakia.
And that evening-- we didn't get very far in Czechoslovakia-- they stopped.
And they said out, not going any further.
So we're left in the middle of nowhere-- strange group, women, children.
We promptly got arrested by the Czech police, who decided that we were German spies.
They took us to the local police station.
They were all drunk-- set us on benches with guns facing us.
It was absolute nightmare.
I mean, they were just completely stupid.
In the morning, as spies, they decided to put us in the hands of the field police, military field police, in another neighboring village.
I can't remember where it was.
In any case, here we struck lucky, because it happened to have been a unit which came back from England.
They were part of the [? free ?] Czechs.
And we told them the truth.
I mean, there was no way we would have gotten away [? with. ?] And they were wonderful.
They gave up their beds in the barracks for our little group.
And they said, OK..
They can't get us to Linz, but they will give us a train pass to go to Pilsen.
Because at that time, Pilsen was in joint hands of the Russians and the Americans.
They said, you've got to get there in the next three days before the Americans move out.
You'll be all right on the train with this pass, but not in Prague.
And we had to change trains in Prague.
In Prague, they won't take any notice of this safe conduct, or whatever you call it.
So we traveled on the train to Prague.
Somehow, I don't know how, we managed to change stations.
And we got to Pilsen, where again, six o'clock in the morning, November, strange city.
Now, how are you going to ask, where are the Americans?
So we just wandered around the town till we saw there was a hotel with an American MP on guard outside, which was a fantastic relief.
So we went in and sat down there.
And we were very lucky, because it turned out that there was a Polish Repatriation Mission in that hotel.
And one of the guys, one of the officers in this mission, knew me from the Warsaw Rising again, recognized me.
So they got us a couple of rooms and some food.
And our two guides set off to Linz to tell my father and his friend who was with him where we were.
And two days later, early in the morning, a 15-ton military truck arrived with my father and his friend and the driver, loaded us.
First time he had seen me since I was nine and 1/2.
And we went from Pilsen to Munich, where we stayed the night in the American Officers' Club.
It was heaven-- continuous donuts and coffee.
[LAUGHS] But Munich was, again, like Warsaw-- complete devastation.
I mean, this was the best hotel the [INAUDIBLE], which was standing, or partly standing.
It was the American Officers' Club.
But the rest of Munich was absolute ruin.
And from then, we drove to Italy-- my father had documents to get us into Italy-- and took us to Milan, where we stayed, I think, for about a week, being dressed and fed and listening to stories.
And all this time, we're asking about George, and was George going to be with us for Christmas?
And my father said, oh, he's in Africa-- maybe.
We'll, you know, it depends.
So we still believed that he was all right.

And after a week, week or 10 days, in Milan, which was absolute heaven-- suddenly, I could go and have a new pair of shoes and clothes, and sleep in a very comfortable bed, and eat as much as I wanted, and have fruits and oranges, and things which I hadn't seen since before the war.
And then we drove south to Porto Recanati, which is near Ancona, where my father's unit was stationed.
And when we arrived, everybody came out to meet us.
And while my mother was talking with my father's fellow officers, I ran upstairs and saw a pile of mail waiting for Father and started looking for a letter from my brother.
I couldn't understand why there wasn't one.
I still remember sort of-- we were stationed in a palazzo, which belonged to the Italian tenor, Beniamino Gigli.
Yes.
And I remember this beautiful staircase.
I was running down.
Father was at the bottom of the stairs.
And I said, no letters from George?
And Father said, be quiet.
And I think my mother heard and turned around.
And he took her upstairs and told us.
He said George had been on the penultimate flight of his second round in July, 1944, when his plane was attacked on the return journey.
It was just before bombers were given a fighter escort across the Channel.
And as he was in a Lancaster, which are very heavy planes, when one of them was attacked, the others just have to go on.
So nobody really knows what happened, see?
But none of the eight-man group were ever found alive.
At first, we had hope that maybe he was somewhere in a prisoner of war camp.
But there has never been any news of that plane, so it's assumed that it's somewhere in the Channel.
So that was a sad part of our reunion.
Thank you.
